q.t: History of the Oriental Orthodox Church
q.h: The Coptic Church Under Islamic Rule — Endurance & Identity
q.quote: The Coptic Patriarch who was challenged by a Fatimid caliph to prove Matthew 17:20 (moving mountains by faith). Coptic tradition names this caliph as al-Muizz li-Dīn Allāh; chronological analysis of Abraham’s patriarchate (975–978) places it under al-Aziz Billah (r. 975–996) — the sources preserve both attributions. When Abraham called the community to three days of fasting and prayer, the Muqattam Mountain reportedly moved visibly three times — witnessed by the Caliph and his court. The Caliph then allowed the Coptic Church significant new freedoms. Abraham is commemorated as a saint on the 29th of Hathor in the Coptic calendar.

q.h: Church Fathers: Deep Dive

q.quote: "A man who hoards the goods of the earth is like one who dams a river — he does not become richer; he only makes those downstream drier."

q.h: Against Despair — Why Fear Must Not Become Paralysis
q.quote: “I saw the snares that the enemy spreads out over the world and I said groaning: ‘What can get through such snares?’ Then I heard a voice saying to me: ‘Humility.’” Anthony does not name correct doctrine, moral achievement, or certainty of election as the path through the snares. He names humility — the posture of one who trusts in God rather than in oneself. This humility is the opposite of both presumption and despair.

q.h: Hesychasm and the Interior Life
q.quote: "The one who sits alone and is quiet has escaped from three wars: hearing, speaking, seeing. But there is one battle he must face — the battle of the heart."

q.h: The Fathers on the Natural Knowledge of God
q.quote: Asked by a visiting philosopher how so great a mind endured the desert without books, Anthony replied: "My book, O philosopher, is the nature of created things, and it is present whenever I wish to read the words of God."

q.h: Voices of the Fathers

q.h: Prayer, Fasting & Almsgiving — The Three Pillars
q.quote: “I saw the snares that the enemy spreads out over the world and I said groaning, ‘What can get through from such snares?’ Then I heard a voice saying to me, ‘Humility.’” Anthony, the father of Christian monasticism, taught that prayer and fasting were tools of humility, not self-achievement. The monastic life was not an escape from Christianity but its intensification.

Athanasius frames the Fall as a slide toward corruption and non-being from which the Word came to rescue us — yet he never teaches that the lost reach annihilation; rather, the unrepentant face "eternal fire" and the judgment of Christ, while the saints receive incorruption. Being held in existence for judgment is itself the work of the Word who upholds all things.
q.cite: On the Incarnation §§4–6; Festal Letters (c. AD 318–360)

q.h: Church Fathers on Assurance
q.quote: “For the Son of God became man so that we might become God.” (On the Incarnation 54). Athanasius’ framing of salvation as theosis — an ongoing participation in the divine life — makes the question of assurance a different question entirely. The issue is not whether one’s ticket has been punched but whether one is genuinely growing in divine life. Assurance is not a one-time declaration but an ongoing reality experienced in prayer and the sacramental life.

q.h: Church Fathers on the Biblical Canon
q.quote: Athanasius’ 39th Festal Letter (367 AD) is the first document to list the exact 27 NT books we have today. For the OT he lists 22 books (following Hebrew enumeration) but immediately adds: “But for greater exactness I add this also, writing of necessity; that there are other books besides these not indeed included in the Canon, but appointed by the Fathers to be read by those who newly join us, and who wish for instruction in the word of godliness: the Wisdom of Solomon, the Wisdom of Sirach, Esther, Judith, Tobias...” His distinction between canonical and “for reading” is often misread as exclusion; it was a pedagogical classification for catechumens, not a rejection.

Athanasius describes the Fall as humanity turning from contemplation of God toward corruption and non-being — a catastrophic sickness of nature that man could not reverse, which is precisely why the Word became flesh: to renew the image of God in man, "as when a portrait has been effaced, the subject must come and sit again so the likeness can be redrawn."

"For the Word, perceiving that no otherwise could the corruption of men be undone save by death as a necessary condition… takes to Himself a body capable of death, that it, by partaking of the Word Who is above all, might be worthy to die in the stead of all (ἀντὶ πάντων)."

"For God has made man thus (that is, as an embodied spirit), and has willed that he should remain in incorruption; but men… gave themselves up to things corruptible… For the soul is made after the image and likeness of God… the way to God lies not far off, nor outside ourselves, but within us, and it is possible to find it from ourselves."

q.t: History of the Oriental Orthodox Church
q.h: The Alexandrian School — Golden Age of Theology
q.quote: “Even if the whole world were against Athanasius, Athanasius is against the world.” (Historia Arianorum). During his third exile, when virtually every bishop in the Empire had signed an Arian formula and he was told “the world is against you,” Athanasius replied: “Then it is Athanasius against the world.” He was recalled to his see after each exile — five times — eventually dying in his own bed, having outlasted every emperor who persecuted him.

q.quote: "Man, having turned from the contemplation of God to evil… fell into the disorder and turbulence of bodily life… Thus he became mortal, and corruption was thenceforth master."

q.quote: "But evil is not from God, nor in God, nor was it in the beginning, nor has it any substantive existence; but men, in default of the vision of good, began to devise and imagine for themselves what was not."

q.h: The Fall: How Death and Natural Evil Entered
q.quote: Transgression set in motion a return to non-existence: "for the transgression of the commandment was making them turn back again according to their nature; and as they had at the beginning come into being out of non-existence, so were they now on the way to returning, through corruption, to non-existence again."

q.h: The Fall: How Death and Natural Evil Entered
q.quote: "Death has become like a tyrant who has been completely conquered … all who believe in Christ tread him under as nought, and choose rather to die than deny their faith in Christ. For they truly know that when they die they are not destroyed, but actually begin to live."

Athanasius argues from a fact his pagan readers could verify with their own eyes: Christians — men, women, and children — now despise death, mocking it as a defeated tyrant, going to martyrdom as to victory. Dead men work no such revolution; the despising of death by thousands is the standing public proof that Christ is risen and death is slain.

q.h: Monarchical Trinitarianism — The Father as Arche
q.quote: "The Son is the Father's radiance; and as the radiance illumines the whole world, so also the Son's Godhead, being one with the Father's, extends to all things and illumines all things visible and intelligible. He is not a second God, nor a God in the sense of a grade or degree; He is God truly and fully, Light truly and fully, for from true Light He is true Light."
q.cite: Against the Arians I.27 — Defending Nicaea through five exiles under four emperors: Athanasius contra mundum

q.h: Creedal Christianity — What Do We Actually Believe?
q.quote: “God became man so that man might become God.” (On the Incarnation 54). Athanasius understood the Creed he helped write at Nicaea not as a boundary fence but as the entry point to participation in divine life. Correct doctrine about Christ is not an end in itself — it is the precondition for the real goal: deification (theosis).

q.h: Creedal Christianity — What Do We Actually Believe?
q.quote: “For the Son of God became man so that we might become God. And he manifested himself by means of a body so that we might perceive the mind of the unseen Father.” (On the Incarnation 54.3). The entire reason for the Incarnation, in Athanasius’ theology, is theosis — making human beings participants in the divine life by taking human nature into the Godhead.

q.h: Creedal Christianity — What Do We Actually Believe?
q.quote: “One must not only read the scriptures, but one must also go to those who practice the truth. For those who practice the truth, their way of life is a proof of the words they have written… whoever sees a genuine Christian sees Christ.” (Life of Anthony). In Athanasius’ account of St. Anthony the Great, the saint’s whole way of life — ascetic, prayerful, humble, healing — is presented as the fullest expression of what it means to be a Christian.

q.h: Apostolic Succession — The Channel of Sacramental Grace
q.quote: "The baptism of Christ, consecrated by the words of the Gospel, is holy, however administered — whether among those who are within or without… nor does it lose its holiness because the one who administers it is a heretic."

q.h: Apostolic Succession — The Channel of Sacramental Grace
q.quote: "The sacrament of baptism is what the person possesses who is baptized; and the sacrament of conferring baptism is what he possesses who is ordained. Either of these sacraments is holy, and can be possessed by a person who is unworthy… but it is one thing to lack the sacrament, another to lack righteousness."
q.cite: c. 400 AD — On Baptism Against the Donatists

q.h: XII. Validity of Orders — The Donatist Question
q.quote: "The sacrament is one thing, the merit of the person administering it is another. The sacrament is holy because of its own origin, not because of the person's holiness. It is administered by good and bad alike; and those who receive it faithfully receive it to their salvation, but those who receive it unfaithfully receive it to their judgment. By this rule, therefore, the person of the minister can neither add to nor take from the power of the sacrament." PARAPHRASE
q.cite: On Baptism, Against the Donatists, Book I — c. AD 400

q.h: XII. Validity of Orders — The Donatist Question
q.quote: "Christ baptizes; Christ absolves; Christ offers the sacrifice. The minister is the instrument in the hands of Christ. Just as a clean hand and a dirty hand can hold the same tool, both equally capable of the same work — so the holy and the sinful priest both hold the same instrument: the ordained office given by Christ." PARAPHRASE
q.cite: Against the Epistle of Parmenianus — c. AD 400

q.h: The Jamnia Dispute — A Post-Christian Jewish Decision
q.quote: “The whole canon of the scriptures, on which we say this discussion should be based, is contained in these books: the five books of Moses...and two books of Maccabees.” (On Christian Doctrine 2.8.13). Augustine explicitly defended the larger Septuagint-based canon and strongly criticized those who would restrict it to the Hebrew list. He noted that the Church’s authority — not rabbinic post-Temple councils — determines the canon.

q.h: The Jamnia Dispute — A Post-Christian Jewish Decision
q.quote: Augustine was the most forceful patristic defender of the larger canon. Against Jerome, he argued that the Greek-speaking Church’s use of the deuterocanonicals for centuries was itself evidence of their authority: “The whole canon of scriptures, however, in which we say this judgment should be based, is contained in these books: Genesis...two books of Maccabees.” (On Christian Doctrine 2.8). He also defended the LXX as inspired, arguing that the Holy Spirit guided the translation just as he guided the original authors.

q.t: History of the Oriental Orthodox Church
q.h: The Syriac Church — From Baghdad's Golden Age to Near Extinction
q.quote: “Wisdom has no nation. Truth has no sect.” Bar Hebraeus was the Maphrian (bishop) of the East and the most encyclopedic scholar of medieval Syriac Christianity. He wrote on theology, philosophy, medicine, grammar, and history — producing a universal chronicle (Chronography) that remains a primary source for medieval Middle Eastern history. He lived through the Mongol destruction of Baghdad and still managed to produce work of extraordinary depth and breadth.

"It is a trick of the devil that many men, as though forgetting these and similar statements of the Lord, ascribe an end to punishment, that they may sin the more boldly. For if there be an end of eternal punishment, eternal life must likewise have an end; and if we may not think this of life, what reason is there to ascribe an end to eternal punishment? q.quote: "Of the beliefs and practices whether generally accepted or publicly enjoined which are preserved in the Church, some we possess derived from written teaching; others we have received delivered to us 'in a mystery' by the tradition of the Apostles; and both of these in relation to true religion have the same force. And these no one will gainsay; — no one, at all events, who is even moderately versed in the institutions of the Church."
q.cite: On the Holy Spirit, Chapter 27 (c. AD 375)

q.h: Indefectibility vs. Infallibility — What the OO Do Affirm
q.quote: "In the case of questions concerning the Faith, it is our duty to refer to no human authority, but to the words of God alone… when the whole world has gone over to heresy, then the man who does not yield knows himself to be preserved by the truth."
q.cite: c. 375 AD — On the Holy Spirit

q.h: What Evil Is — The Privation of Good
q.quote: "Evil is not a living and animated substance, but a condition of the soul which is opposed to virtue, and which springs up in the careless on account of their falling away from good. Do not, then, look upon evil as having an existence of its own, nor imagine that there is some original evil nature."

q.h: Voices of the Fathers
q.quote: He taught that hoarding is a form of theft: the bread you store belongs to the hungry, the cloak in your chest to the naked, the silver buried in the ground to the poor. You wrong as many as you might have helped, and did not.

q.h: XIII.
q.quote: "Whoever is in trouble, let him have recourse to these martyrs; whoever is in joy, let him come to them also. The former, so that he may find relief from his pains; the latter, so that his good fortune may be preserved. Here a pious woman prays for her children; let her approach the martyrs. Here a man intercedes for the healing of his diseases; let the martyrs be his helpers."
q.cite: Homily on the Forty Martyrs of Sebaste (c. AD 374)

q.t: Salvation in the Oriental Orthodox Faith
q.h: XI. The Patristic Witness — Salvation Through the Ages
q.quote: "Baptism is the ransom of captives, the remission of debts, the death of sin, the regeneration of the soul, the garment of light, the holy and indissoluble seal, the chariot to heaven, the delights of paradise, the pledge of the kingdom, the grace of adoption."

q.h: VII. The Church Fathers on Faith and Salvation
q.quote: "Faith is not a bare assent of the mind — it is a living trust that shapes the whole person: thought, desire, and action. He who truly believes in Christ does what Christ commanded. The faith that does not produce obedience and love is not yet the faith that saves." PARAPHRASE

q.h: III. The Oriental Orthodox View — Scripture Within Tradition
q.quote: "Of the beliefs and practices whether generally accepted or publicly enjoined which are preserved in the Church, some we possess derived from written teaching; others we have received delivered to us in a mystery by the tradition of the apostles; and both of these in relation to true religion have the same force."
q.cite: On the Holy Spirit, Chapter 27 — c. AD 375

q.h: Monarchical Trinitarianism — The Father as Arche
q.quote: "We confess that the nature of the Holy Trinity is simple, without composition, without parts, indivisible. We confess the Father as the source and cause; the Son as begotten from the Father; the Holy Spirit as proceeding from the Father through the Son. But we do not confess three origins, or three Goods, or three Gods; we confess one Goodness, one God, one First Principle."
q.cite: Letter 189 — The Cappadocian consolidation of Nicene theology

q.h: Prayer, Fasting & Almsgiving — The Three Pillars
q.quote: “The bread which you do not use is the bread of the hungry; the garment hanging in your wardrobe is the garment of the naked; the shoes that you do not wear are the shoes of the one who is barefoot; the money that you keep locked away is the money of the poor.” Basil founded the first systematic Christian hospital and wrote rules for monastic communities that shaped all subsequent Eastern and Western monasticism.

q.h: Church Fathers on the Biblical Canon
q.quote: Clement quotes almost every deuterocanonical book as scripture, often with the formula “the divine scripture says.” He quotes 1 Enoch as inspired prophecy. His Stromata cites Wisdom more than almost any canonical book outside the Psalms and Proverbs. The Alexandrian school Clement founded — which produced Origen, Athanasius, and Cyril — was shaped by this broad canonical inheritance.

Clement, head of Alexandria's catechetical school, teaches throughout the Stromata that faith is a free assent of the soul cooperating with God's drawing, that God's commands address self-determining creatures, and that salvation perfects rather than overrides the rational freedom in which man images God.

q.h: III. Abortion — The Sanctity of Life from Conception
q.quote: "Our whole life can go on in observation of the laws of nature, if we gain dominion over our desires from the beginning and if we do not kill, by various means of a perverse art, the human offspring, born according to the designs of divine providence."

q.h: III. Abortion — The Sanctity of Life from Conception
q.quote: "The human ideal of continence, I mean that which is set forth by Greek philosophers, teaches that one should fight desire and not be subservient to it so as to bring it to practical effect. But our ideal is not to experience desire at all... especially that desire which seeks to pervert nature."

Clement teaches that creation did not occur in time as we know it — time itself was created with the world — and that the six days express order and rank in creation rather than a mere succession of hours.

q.t: Aisha's Age, Early Marriage & the Bible
q.h: VI.
q.quote: "Marriage is the first conjunction of man and woman for the procreation of legitimate children. And so the family is the first community — not merely a biological unit but a rational and spiritual one, formed by willing persons under God."

q.h: Voices of the Fathers
q.quote: "Riches, then, which benefit also our neighbours, are not to be thrown away… Are you able to make a right use of it? It is subservient to righteousness. Does one make a wrong use of it? It is, on the other hand, a minister of wrong. For its nature is to be subservient, not to rule."

q.t: Salvation in the Oriental Orthodox Faith
q.h: IX. Theosis — The Goal of Salvation
q.quote: "The Logos of God had become man so that you might learn from man how man may become God."

q.h: Is Christianity Just Belief?
q.quote: “The true Gnostic [i.e., mature Christian] prays throughout his entire life. He tries by prayer to have a fellowship with God… he is no longer the slave of pleasure, but the friend of God.” (Stromata 7.7). Clement describes Christian maturity as a state of continuous prayer and divine friendship — not a point reached but a mode of existence continuously inhabited.

q.h: VIII. Clement of Rome — The First Witness
q.quote: "The apostles have preached the Gospel to us from the Lord Jesus Christ; Jesus Christ has done so from God. Christ therefore was sent forth by God, and the apostles by Christ. Both these appointments, then, were made in an orderly way, according to the will of God. Having therefore received their orders, and being fully assured by the resurrection of our Lord Jesus Christ, and established in the word of God, with full assurance of the Holy Ghost, they went forth proclaiming that the kingdom of God was at hand. And thus preaching through countries and cities, they appointed the first-fruits of their labours, having first proved them by the Spirit, to be bishops and deacons of those who should afterwards believe."

q.h: VIII. Clement of Rome — The First Witness
q.quote: "Our apostles also knew, through our Lord Jesus Christ, that there would be strife on account of the office of the episcopate. For this reason, therefore, inasmuch as they had obtained a perfect fore-knowledge of this, they appointed those already mentioned, and afterwards gave instructions, that when these should fall asleep, other approved men should succeed them in their ministry… Those, therefore, who were appointed by them, or afterwards by other eminent men, with the consent of the whole Church, and who have blamelessly served the flock of Christ in a humble, peaceable, and disinterested spirit… we consider to be unjustly thrust out from their ministrations."

q.h: Church Fathers on the Biblical Canon
q.quote: In his letter to the Corinthians (1 Clement, c. 96 AD), Clement quotes Judith as an example of heroic faith: “The blessed Judith, when her city was in danger, asked of the elders that she might go out into the camp of the strangers; and exposing herself to danger, she went forth for the love of her country and her people.” (1 Clement 55.4–5). He treats Judith not as an interesting story but as a normative scriptural example.

Clement, writing from Rome in the first century, says the apostles, foreseeing strife over the bishop's office, appointed their first converts as bishops and deacons and provided that when these died, other approved men should succeed to their ministry — and rebukes Corinth for deposing clergy appointed in this succession.

"Having therefore received their orders, and being fully assured by the resurrection of our Lord Jesus Christ, and established in the word of God, with full assurance of the Holy Ghost, they went forth proclaiming that the kingdom of God was at hand."

q.h: VII. The Church Fathers on Faith and Salvation
q.quote: "Let us therefore cleave to His blessing, and consider what are the means of possessing it. Let us think over the things which have taken place from the beginning. For what reason was our father Abraham blessed? Was it not because he wrought righteousness and truth through faith?"

q.h: The Cyprian–Augustine Debate — How the Tradition Settled This
q.quote: "Baptism cannot exist outside the Church, since there is one baptism, and it exists in the Catholic Church alone… How can one who is not himself holy give holiness? How can one who has not the Holy Spirit give the Holy Spirit?"
q.cite: c. 255 AD — On the Rebaptism of Heretics

q.quote: "He can no longer have God for his Father, who has not the Church for his mother."
q.cite: On the Unity of the Church 6 (c. AD 251)

q.t: The Authorial Power of the Church
q.h: Matthew 18:15–20 — Binding and Loosing
q.quote: "He cannot have God as his Father who does not have the Church as his Mother… Whoever is separated from the Church is joined to an adulteress."
q.cite: c. 250 AD — On the Unity of the Church

q.h: Church Practices
q.quote: "How much more should we not resist little ones from receiving the Eucharist? Who among us can say they are unworthy to receive the grace that Christ himself offered to all?"

q.h: VI. Witness of the Church Fathers
q.quote: "But in respect of the case of the infants, which you say ought not to be baptized within the second or third day after their birth… we all thought very differently in our council. For in this course no one of us thought that there was anything to be followed but the mercy and grace of God… we ought not to hinder any person from baptism and the grace of God. This is to be observed and maintained for all, but especially for infants and newly-born persons."

q.h: XII.
q.quote: "If Christ Jesus, our Lord and God, is himself the High Priest of God the Father, and has first offered himself a sacrifice to the Father, and has commanded this to be done in commemoration of himself, certainly that priest truly discharges the office of Christ, who imitates that which Christ did; and he then offers a true and full sacrifice in the Church to God the Father, when he proceeds to offer it according to what he sees Christ himself to have offered."

q.h: The Apostolic College — Shared Authority from the Beginning
q.quote: "The episcopate is one, each part of which is held by each one for the whole… the apostleship is one and undivided; and he [Christ] gave to all the apostles equal power and dignity."
q.cite: c. 251 AD — On the Unity of the Church

q.h: XIII.
q.quote: "Let us remember one another in concord and unanimity. Let us on both sides always pray for one another. Let us relieve burdens and afflictions by mutual love, that if one of us, by the swiftness of divine condescension, shall go hence the first, our love may continue in the presence of the Lord."

q.h: The Church as Body of Christ
q.quote: “He can no longer have God for his Father who has not the Church for his mother. He who gathers elsewhere than in the Church, scatters the Church of Christ.” (On the Unity of the Church 6). Cyprian’s famous formulation — “Outside the Church there is no salvation” — was not a declaration of condemnation but a statement about where the life of God is normatively found: in the Body of Christ.

q.t: History of the Oriental Orthodox Church
q.h: The Coptic Church Under Islamic Rule — Endurance & Identity
q.quote: Cyril II served during the Fatimid period and worked to maintain the church’s institutional life during a period of internal disorder and the beginning of Fatimid decline. His pontificate established administrative practices — the systematic collection of church revenues, the organization of episcopal sees — that helped the church survive the coming Mamluk era. His pastoral letters reveal a church under constant economic pressure from the jizya and land confiscations.

q.h: Church Fathers on Assurance
q.quote: “Let no man be over-confident in himself concerning salvation, nor yet disheartened at the greatness of his sins. For God is full of compassion, readily pardons those who repent, and visits those who persist in sin with correction.” Cyril, the great Patriarch of Alexandria and defender of the Theotokos, holds the exact balance: neither over-confidence nor despondency. The ground is God’s compassion — which is both a reason for hope and a reason not to abuse it.

q.h: Church Fathers on the Biblical Canon
q.quote: Patriarch of Alexandria and the great defender of Theotokos, Cyril regularly cites Wisdom and Sirach as inspired scripture in his Christological writings. His commentary on John draws heavily on Wisdom’s Logos theology. As the leading theologian of the Alexandrian tradition that gave us the Coptic Church, his acceptance of these books is foundational for Oriental Orthodox canon tradition.

Cyril teaches that God's foreknowledge of who would believe precedes, in order of reason, His predestining: God "predestined those whom He foreknew" — knowledge of free response, not arbitrary decree, is the basis of the call.

Cyril explains the Father's drawing as worked through revelation and teaching that wins the soul — a drawing that operates by illumination and persuasion rather than violence, for God "does all things gently and by counsel" with rational creatures.
q.cite: Commentary on John, Book 4, on 6:44 (c. AD 428)

q.quote: Cyril teaches that the branch genuinely in the Vine can, through its own negligence and refusal to remain, wither and be cast out — the warning of Christ is real, not hypothetical; remaining in Christ engages the believer's continuing will and love.

q.h: Cyril's Formula — The Foundation of Oriental Orthodox Christology
q.quote: "The holy body of Christ was his very own, not as being inseparable by some conjunction of dignity or authority, nor as if some divine indwelling had taken place, as in the case of the prophets; rather it was his own in a true sense, since he who transcends all things is said to have been born according to the flesh."

q.h: Cyril's Formula — The Foundation of Oriental Orthodox Christology
q.quote: "We confess, therefore, our Lord Jesus Christ, the Son of God, the Only-begotten, perfect God and perfect man of rational soul and body… the same one Christ, one Son, one Lord."

q.h: III.
q.quote: "We do not say that the nature of the Word was changed and became flesh, or that it was converted into a whole man consisting of soul and body; but rather that the Word, having personally united to himself flesh animated by a rational soul, did in an ineffable and inconceivable manner become man."

q.h: III.
q.quote: "I am amazed that there are some who are entirely in doubt as to whether the holy Virgin should be called Theotokos or not. For if our Lord Jesus Christ is God, how is the holy Virgin who bore him not the Mother of God?"
q.cite: Letter to the Monks of Egypt (Letter 1, AD 429)

q.quote: Cyril's constant theme: the Word of God did not merely dwell in a man named Jesus — the Word Himself became man, so that Mary is rightly called Theotokos, "Birth-giver of God," and the one who suffered on the Cross is the Word in His own flesh. His watchword, "one incarnate nature of God the Word," remains the banner of the Oriental Orthodox confession.
q.cite: Letters and treatises against Nestorius (c. AD 430)

q.h: Saints and Mary
q.quote: "If anyone does not confess that Emmanuel is truly God, and that therefore the holy Virgin is Theotokos — for she bore according to the flesh the Word of God made flesh — let him be anathema."

q.h: Saints and Mary
q.quote: "We do not divide the one Lord Jesus Christ into two; one Son before the Incarnation and another after — we confess one and the same Son, our Lord Jesus Christ, the same before and after the Incarnation."

q.h: III. Theotokos — The Title and Its Christological Meaning
q.quote: "We confess that the holy, ever-virgin Mary is the Mother of God, inasmuch as the divine Word was made flesh and became man and from the very conception united to himself the temple taken from her."

q.h: III. Theotokos — The Title and Its Christological Meaning
q.quote: "We acknowledge the holy Virgin to be at once the Mother of God and the ever-virgin (Aeiparthenos); for the One who was incarnate of her is the eternal Word."

q.t: History of the Oriental Orthodox Church
q.h: The Alexandrian School — Golden Age of Theology
q.quote: “One is Christ the Son and Lord, not as if a man were merely joined to God by a unity of dignity and authority… but a true union by which the Word, by his Incarnation, is God and man at once.” (On the Unity of Christ). This formulation — one nature of the incarnate Word (mia physis) — is the theological cornerstone of miaphysitism. The Council of Ephesus vindicated Cyril completely in 431.

q.h: The Eastern Understanding: Ancestral Sin
q.quote: "We have become sinners through the disobedience of Adam in this sense: through his disobedience, sin entered the world and death through sin, and so death spread to all men."

q.h: The OO Reading of Matthew 16 — Patristic Evidence
q.quote: "The unshaken rock is the inviolable faith of Peter… Whoever possesses this faith, he too shall be a rock."
q.cite: c. 428 AD — On the Gospel of John

q.h: The Cross: God's Answer in Person
q.quote: "Whosoever shall not confess that the Word of God suffered in the flesh, and was crucified in the flesh, and tasted death in the flesh … let him be anathema."

q.h: The Immaculate Conception of Mary
q.quote: "Hail, Mary Theotokos, the spotless jewel of the whole world, the lamp that is never quenched, the crown of virginity, the scepter of orthodoxy."

q.t: Salvation in the Oriental Orthodox Faith
q.h: IX. Theosis — The Goal of Salvation
q.quote: "We are made partakers of the divine nature through participation in the Holy Spirit."

q.h: VII. The Church Fathers on Faith and Salvation
q.quote: "When he says 'this is the work of God, that you believe in him whom he has sent,' he does not exclude all work but shows that the work which God requires and approves is faith in Christ — a faith which then expresses itself through love and obedience. For faith in Christ is itself a comprehensive act of the whole person turning to God."

q.h: Theotokos — The Council of Ephesus (431)
q.quote: "I am amazed that any doubt should have arisen about whether the Holy Virgin should be called Theotokos. For if our Lord Jesus Christ is God, how is the Holy Virgin who bore him not Theotokos?"

q.h: Theotokos — The Council of Ephesus (431)
q.quote: "Hail, Mary Theotokos, for through you the Trinity is glorified… through you the fallen creature is lifted up to heaven."

q.h: The Eternal Procession of the Holy Spirit
q.quote: "The Godhead is not added to or taken from; it is entirely in the Son and entirely in the Holy Spirit, just as it is entire in the Father. The Father gives the whole of Himself to the Son; the Son receives the whole of the Father in generation; the Spirit proceeds bearing the whole of the Father's Godhead. There are not three portions of the one Godhead, but one Godhead entirely possessed by each of three."
q.cite: Thesaurus de Sancta et Consubstantiali Trinitate — The most exhaustive Trinitarian treatise from Alexandria after Athanasius

q.quote: "We shall be raised therefore, all with our bodies eternal, but not all with bodies alike: for if a man is righteous, he will receive a heavenly body… but if a man is a sinner, he shall receive an eternal body, fitted to endure the penalties of sins, that he may burn eternally in fire, nor ever be consumed."

q.h: The Jamnia Dispute — A Post-Christian Jewish Decision
q.quote: “Read the divine scriptures, the twenty-two books of the Old Testament...But read also the two books of Esdras, and Tobit, and Judith, and Maccabees.” (Catechetical Lectures 4.35). Cyril distinguished the deuterocanonicals slightly but still directed catechumens to read them as part of their scriptural formation.

q.h: The Jamnia Dispute — A Post-Christian Jewish Decision
q.quote: “Of the New Testament there are four Gospels only, for the rest have false titles...The Revelation of John is likewise left by many outside our canonical books, but if it be judged proper, it may be received; and this judgment I leave to the Church of each place.” (Catechetical Lectures 4.36)
"Know also that the soul is self-governed (αὐτεξούσιος)… The soul has free will: and the devil indeed can suggest, but he has not the power to compel against the will."
"…the Lamb of God who takes away the sin of the world… He did not suffer for a few, but the world was saved through His suffering."

q.h: Saints and Mary
q.quote: "We make mention also of those who have fallen asleep before us… We pray for them believing it will be of great benefit to the souls for whom the prayer is offered."

q.h: Saints and Mary
q.quote: "Do not think of the bread and wine as mere bread and wine; they are the body and blood of Christ as the Lord himself declared. Even if your senses suggest otherwise, faith makes you certain."

q.quote: "In the same moment you died and were born; the saving water was both your grave and your mother."

q.h: Saints and Mary
q.quote: "After you came up from the pool of sacred baptism, you received the anointing, the antitype of that with which Christ was anointed — and this is the Holy Spirit."

q.h: Saints and Mary
q.quote: "Read the divine Scriptures, both the Old and New Testaments… and whatever is not read in the churches, that read not even by yourself."

q.h: Saints and Mary
q.quote: "Then we pray also for the holy fathers and bishops who have fallen asleep, and in general for all who have fallen asleep before us, believing it will be of great benefit."

q.h: XIII.
q.quote: "How is it that the bones of Elisha raised a dead man? Why do the garments of the Apostles heal the sick? Is it that the grace of God dwells even in bodies and physical things? Certainly. For the Holy Spirit, who comes upon the water of Baptism and upon the oil of Chrismation, also dwells in the bodies of the saints — and their relics and bones, by his indwelling, retain their sanctifying power."

q.t: The Sacred Liturgy of the Early Church
q.h: X. Hippolytus — The Apostolic Tradition (c. AD 215)
q.quote: "After this the priest cries aloud, 'Lift up your hearts.' Then you answer, 'We lift them up unto the Lord.' Then the priest says, 'Let us give thanks to the Lord.' Then you say, 'It is meet and right.' Then we make mention of heaven, and earth, and sea, and sun, and moon, and stars, and all the creation... Then we commemorate also those who have fallen asleep before us... Then after the spiritual sacrifice is perfected, the bloodless service... we call upon God for the common peace of the Churches, for the welfare of the world..."

q.h: III.
q.quote: "At the same moment you were dying and being born; and that water of salvation was at once your grave and your mother. What Solomon said in another context is applicable to you: 'There is a time to give birth, and a time to die' — but for you, the order is reversed: a time to die, and a time to be born; and both times happened in the self-same moment."

q.h: III.
q.quote: "After being baptized into Christ, and having put on Christ, you have been made conformable to the Son of God. For God, having predestinated us to the adoption of sons, made us like the glorious Body of Christ. Being therefore made partakers of Christ, you are properly called Christs, and of you God said, 'Touch not My Christs.' Now you were made Christs, by receiving the antitype of the Holy Ghost."

q.h: Voices of the Fathers
q.quote: "…if a king were to banish certain who had given him offense, and then those who belong to them should weave a crown and offer it to him on behalf of those under punishment, would he not grant a remission of their penalties? In the same way we, when we offer to Him our supplications for those who have fallen asleep… [are] propitiating our merciful God for them."

q.t: Salvation in the Oriental Orthodox Faith
q.h: IV. Baptismal Regeneration — The Biblical Case
q.quote: "If any man receive not baptism, he hath not salvation; except only martyrs, who even without the water receive the kingdom. For when the Saviour, in redeeming the world by his cross, was pierced in the side, he shed forth blood and water; that men, living in times of peace, might be baptized in water, and, in times of persecution, be baptized in their own blood."

q.t: Salvation in the Oriental Orthodox Faith
q.h: IV. Baptismal Regeneration — The Biblical Case
q.quote: "Think not that Baptism brings only the grace of remission of sins... but Baptism is the beginning and source of divine gifts."

q.h: XIII. Cessationism vs. Continuationism — The OO Position
q.quote: "The Holy Spirit gives different gifts to different people in view of the profit of each one, as he wills... One man receives the gift of wisdom, another receives faith, another receives gifts of healing."

q.h: Baptism — The New Birth
q.quote: “If a man receive not Baptism, he hath not salvation; except only Martyrs, who even without the water receive the kingdom. For when the Savior says, ‘Unless a man be born of water and of the Spirit, he cannot enter the Kingdom of God,’ those who are not baptized are excluded from the Kingdom.” (Catechetical Lectures 3.4)

q.h: Before Chalcedon — The Road That Led There
q.quote: "I accept 'of two natures' but not 'in two natures.' The difference is this: 'of two' means they came together in union; 'in two' means they remain separate after the union."
q.cite: c. 449 AD — At the Robber Council

q.h: VII.
q.quote: "I accept the formula of Cyril: one nature of God the Logos incarnate. I do not accept the novelty of two natures after the union. To speak of two natures after the union is to divide Christ and to follow Nestorius. I stand with Nicaea, Constantinople, and Ephesus."

q.t: History of the Oriental Orthodox Church
q.h: The Council of Chalcedon (451) — The Defining Schism
q.quote: “Though I am in exile, I am not alone. The same Christ who was exiled with the prophets is exiled with me.” (words attributed to Dioscorus in exile). His three years of exile at Gangra, maintaining his theological position without recanting, established the pattern of holy resistance that the OO churches would follow through every subsequent persecution.

q.h: Church Fathers on Assurance
q.quote: “I am not worthy even to call myself a sinner. A sinner at least sins; I have added iniquity to iniquity all my days.” Ephrem — one of the greatest hymn-writers and theologians of the Syriac church, venerated in both OO and EO traditions — expressed himself with a radical humility that was not theatrical but genuine. His Prayer of St. Ephrem (recited throughout Orthodox Lent) asks only for self-knowledge and repentance — not for assurance of one’s standing.

Ephrem sings that God places the key of paradise in the hand of human freedom: grace surrounds, woos, and adorns the soul, but the door of the bridal chamber is opened from within. Adam fell by free choice; the second Adam restores the choice and awaits its "yes."
q.cite: Hymns on Paradise & Hymns on Faith (c. AD 360)

q.quote: Ephrem sings that God planted the Tree as a contest for Adam's freedom: the command was light, the choice real, the fall free — and the door of Paradise reopens to freedom restored in Christ, for God "does not compel, He who is the Lord of freedom."
"So let no one think that there is anything allegorical in the works of the six days. No one can rightly say that the things that pertain to these days were symbolic."

q.h: Saints and Mary
q.quote: "You alone and your Mother are more beautiful than everything; for there is no blemish in you, nor any stain in your Mother."

q.h: Saints and Mary
q.quote: "Remember me in your prayers, that the mercy of God may be shown to me in the day of judgment… The saints do not forget us; they pray for us unceasingly."

q.quote: "Who is able to speak of the Mighty One? Who can describe the Mother of God (Yāldaṯ Allāhā)?" — The Syriac churches used the equivalent title independently of the Greek tradition.

q.h: VI. Patristic Witness to Theotokos
q.quote: Celebrates Mary as the virgin who conceived and bore without losing her virginity — a recurring theme of the Syriac Nativity hymns sung in the Oriental tradition.
q.cite: c. AD 370 · Hymns on the Nativity Paraphrase

q.h: Purgatory

q.h: XIII.

q.t: Salvation in the Oriental Orthodox Faith
q.h: XI. The Patristic Witness — Salvation Through the Ages
q.quote: "In your fount, O Lord, I have found a paradise... Blessed is the baptism with whose white robe the soul is clothed and the stain of guilt is cleansed."

q.h: Panagia — The All-Holy
q.quote: "You alone and your Mother are more beautiful than everything; for there is no blemish in you, nor any stain in your Mother. Who of my children can compare in beauty to these?"

q.h: The Apophatic Limit — What Cannot Be Said
q.quote: "Holy, Holy, Holy: these three are one Lord. The voice that says ‘Holy’ once is the voice of a single mouth; the voice that says ‘Holy’ three times is the voice of a single truth about a threefold mystery. Blessed is He who has concealed His simplicity within the richness of His persons, and who has made His persons to shine within the light of His simplicity."
q.cite: Hymns on Faith 73 — Ephrem wrote in Syriac; his Trinitarian hymns remain central to Syriac Orthodox worship to this day

q.h: II. Key Terms — Glossary

q.h: The Ethiopian Canon — 81 Books
q.quote: The apostle of Ethiopia, consecrated by Athanasius of Alexandria, brought Christianity to Aksum. Under his influence the Aksumite kingdom became Christian under King Ezana. The Ethiopian canon was shaped in this Alexandrian theological environment, inheriting the broad Alexandrian scriptural tradition that included 1 Enoch and Jubilees already valued in the pre-Christian Beta Israel community.

q.t: History of the Oriental Orthodox Church
q.h: Ethiopia — Ahmad Gran and the Islamic Jihad (1529–1543)
q.quote: The emperor who defeated Ahmad Gran and then spent his reign rebuilding the Ethiopian church. He wrote a theological treatise — the Confessio Claudii — defending Ethiopian Orthodox doctrine against Portuguese Jesuit missionaries who had arrived with the military alliance and immediately began attempting to convert Ethiopia to Roman Catholicism. Gelawdewos’ treatise is a remarkable instance of a monarch personally defending his church’s theology in writing.

q.h: Palamism and the Uncreated Energies
q.quote: "God in his entirety penetrates the whole of the deified being, and the whole of the deified being is permeated by God… The saints are truly united to God, not to some created intermediary."

q.h: VI. Eastern Theological Objections
q.quote: "We do not say that the Spirit is from the Son or through the Son in terms of his eternal procession, his hypostatic existence. But we say that the Spirit is given through the Son, manifested through the Son, shines forth through the Son — in the economy of salvation. These are different things: eternal procession and temporal mission. The filioque confuses what must be kept distinct." PARAPHRASE
q.cite: Apodictic Treatises on the Procession of the Holy Spirit — c. AD 1335

q.h: Created Grace vs. Uncreated Energies
q.quote: "God in his entirety penetrates the whole of the deified being… The saints are truly united to God, not to some created intermediary."

q.quote: Gregory’s famous poem on the scriptural canon (Carmen 1.12) lists 26 NT books — omitting only Revelation. His list ends: “You have all. If anything is outside these, it is not among the genuine.” Yet Gregory’s judgment on this point was not final: the Church ultimately received the book.

q.h: X. The Fathers Against Determinism
q.quote: Gregory teaches that salvation must be willed as well as given — God does not save the unwilling by force — and exhorts the baptized to guard the gift, since the illumination received can be darkened by a returning life of sin.

q.h: Evidence 2 — Why Anything Exists at All
q.quote: "For how could this universe have come into being or been put together, unless God had called it into existence, and held it together? For every one who sees a beautifully made lute, and considers the skill with which it has been fitted together and arranged, or who hears its melody, would think of none but the lutemaker, or the luteplayer, and would recur to him in mind, though he might not know him by sight."

q.quote: "If anyone does not acknowledge Mary as Theotokos, he is without the Godhead."

q.h: The Immaculate Conception of Mary

q.quote: "Baptism is God's most beautiful and magnificent gift... We call it gift, grace, anointing, enlightenment, garment of immortality, bath of regeneration, seal, and most precious gift. It is called gift because it is given to those who bring nothing of their own."

q.h: Panagia — The All-Holy
q.quote: "If anyone does not accept Mary as Theotokos, he is cut off from the Godhead. If anyone says she is Theotokos but treats her as a mere vessel, without reverence, he too has missed the point."

q.h: One God, Three Persons — The Foundation
q.quote: "Believe that God is one and alone, unbegotten; and has a Son who is begotten, but not like human sons; and has a Spirit who proceeds — not like winds. These three are one God. But the principle of unity is the Father, from whom the Son is begotten and the Spirit proceeds, and towards whom they are united. The distinction is of persons; the unity is of essence."

q.h: Creedal Christianity — What Do We Actually Believe?
q.quote: “Let us theologize in a spirit of reverence. We are not talking about a doctrine we have invented; we are confronting a reality that exceeds us.” The Cappadocian Fathers who expanded the Creed at Constantinople (381) approached the task with awe rather than confidence — aware that the Creed gestures toward a mystery that no formula exhausts.

Gregory teaches that Christ's death was offered for the whole of human nature: in assuming our common nature, the Word's saving work touches the entire race, "the lost sheep" being all of humanity carried home on the Shepherd's shoulders.

"We are in some manner our own parents, giving birth to ourselves by our own free choice in accordance with whatever we wish to be, whether male or female, moulding ourselves to the teaching of virtue or vice."

q.h: Part I — The Fathers on the Conquest
q.quote: Troubled by the death of the Egyptian firstborn read literally — "how would the punishment of the child be just?" — Gregory teaches that the passage commands us to destroy the first beginnings of evil in ourselves before they grow.

q.h: Part I — The Fathers on the Conquest

q.h: Part II — The Fathers Against Slavery

q.quote: "He who made man for the participation of His own peculiar good … would never have deprived him of that most excellent and precious of all goods — I mean the gift of liberty and self-determination. He recognized the woman as the Mother of the Lord, and the old man as John the Evangelist. The blessed Virgin commanded John to reveal to Gregory the mystery of true faith."

q.quote: "The soul... rising always from perfection to perfection... never comes to a ceiling in its growth toward God. Such is the true meaning of seeing God: never to have this desire satisfied."

q.h: Monarchical Trinitarianism — The Father as Arche
q.quote: "One light, three radiances. One wisdom, three forms of knowing. One goodness, three modes of giving. One life, three modes of living. The Father lives; the Son lives from the Father; the Spirit lives from the Father through the Son. They are not three lives, but one life in three, undivided and inseparable, yet genuinely three."
q.cite: On Not Three Gods — The definitive resolution of why Trinitarian theology is not tritheism

q.h: Primacy of Honor vs. Primacy of Jurisdiction — The Central Distinction

q.h: Apostolic Origins — How Each Church Was Founded
q.quote: “If we call ourselves Christians, let us do as Christ did.” Gregory, imprisoned for 13 years in a pit (Khor Virap — “deep dungeon”) by the king he would eventually convert, emerged to baptize King Tiridates and transform Armenia forever. He is the patron saint of Armenia and the founding father of the Armenian church. The Khor Virap monastery built over his prison pit is now the most visited site in Armenia, with Mount Ararat visible in the background.

"Do not err, my brethren. Those that corrupt families shall not inherit the kingdom of God… he who corrupts the faith of God, for which Jesus Christ was crucified, shall go away into everlasting fire, and so shall every one that hearkens unto him."

q.h: IX. Ignatius of Antioch — The Martyr Bishop
q.quote: "See that you all follow the bishop, even as Jesus Christ does the Father, and the presbytery as you would the apostles; and reverence the deacons, as being the institution of God. Let no man do anything connected with the Church without the bishop. Let that be deemed a proper Eucharist, which is either under the bishop's presidency or the person to whom he shall entrust it. Wherever the bishop appears, there let the multitude also be; just as, wherever Jesus Christ is, there is the Catholic Church. It is not lawful to baptize or give communion without the consent of the bishop; but whatever he shall approve of, that is also pleasing to God."
q.cite: Letter to the Smyrnaeans, Chapter 8 — c. AD 107

q.h: IX. Ignatius of Antioch — The Martyr Bishop
q.quote: "It is fitting, O Ephesians, that you should be in perfect harmony with the mind of the bishop, as indeed you are. Your admirable presbytery, worthy of God, is fitted as exactly to the bishop as the strings are to the harp. Therefore in your concord and harmonious love, Jesus Christ is sung. And do ye, man by man, become a choir, that being harmonious in love, and taking up the song of God in unison, ye may with one voice sing to the Father through Jesus Christ, so that He may both hear you, and perceive by your works that ye are indeed the members of His Son."
q.cite: Letter to the Ephesians, Chapter 4–5 — c. AD 107

q.h: IX. Ignatius of Antioch — The Martyr Bishop
q.quote: "In like manner, let all reverence the deacons as an appointment of Jesus Christ, and the bishop as Jesus Christ, who is the Son of the Father, and the presbyters as the sanhedrim of God, and assembly of the apostles. Apart from these, there is no Church."
q.cite: Letter to the Trallians, Chapter 3 — c. AD 107

q.h: The Church as Body of Christ
q.quote: “Where the bishop is present, there let the congregation gather, just as where Jesus Christ is, there is the Catholic Church.” (Letter to the Smyrnaeans 8.2). Writing on his way to martyrdom in Rome, Ignatius makes the local bishop-led Eucharistic assembly the criterion of authentic Christianity. He is the first writer to use the phrase “Catholic Church” (meaning universal, undivided).

q.h: The Church as Body of Christ
q.quote: “Christianity is not a matter of persuasion, but of greatness, especially when it is hated by the world. Let us not merely call ourselves Christians, but be Christians.” (Letter to the Magnesians 4). Writing on his way to be executed in Rome, Ignatius distinguishes the claim of the name from the reality. His letters — among the earliest Christian writings outside the NT — constantly emphasize Eucharist, unity with the bishop, and the concrete assembly of the Church.

q.h: Apostolic Succession — The Channel of Sacramental Grace
q.quote: "It is within the power of all… to reckon up those who were by the apostles instituted bishops in the churches, and the successors of these men to our own times… True knowledge is the doctrine of the apostles, and the ancient constitution of the Church throughout all the world."

q.h: X. Irenaeus of Lyons — The Great Defender
q.quote: "It is within the power of all, therefore, in every Church, who may wish to see the truth, to contemplate clearly the tradition of the apostles manifested throughout the whole world; and we are in a position to reckon up those who were by the apostles instituted bishops in the Churches, and to demonstrate the succession of these men to our own times; those who neither taught nor knew of anything like what these heretics rave about. For if the apostles had known hidden mysteries, which they were in the habit of imparting to 'the perfect' apart and privily from the rest, they would have delivered them especially to those to whom they were also committing the Churches themselves."
q.cite: Against Heresies, Book III, Chapter 3 — c. AD 180

q.h: X. Irenaeus of Lyons — The Great Defender
q.quote: "The blessed apostles, then, having founded and built up the Church, committed into the hands of Linus the office of the episcopate. Of this Linus, Paul makes mention in the Epistles to Timothy. To him succeeded Anacletus; and after him, in the third place from the apostles, Anicetus received the episcopate, of whom Paul also makes mention. Him Soter followed, and after him Eleutherius does now, in the twelfth place from the apostles, hold the inheritance of the episcopate. In this order, and by this succession, the ecclesiastical tradition from the apostles, and the preaching of the truth, have come down to us."
q.cite: Against Heresies, Book III, Chapter 3 — c. AD 180

q.h: X. Irenaeus of Lyons — The Great Defender
q.quote: "Wherefore it is incumbent to obey the presbyters who are in the Church — those who, as I have shown, possess the succession from the apostles; those who, together with the succession of the episcopate, have received the certain gift of truth, according to the good pleasure of the Father. But it is also incumbent to hold in suspicion others who depart from the primitive succession, and assemble themselves together in any place whatsoever."
q.cite: Against Heresies, Book IV, Chapter 26 — c. AD 180

He also quotes Wisdom as “the scripture.”

"God made man a free [agent] from the beginning, possessing his own power, even as he does his own soul, to obey the behests of God voluntarily, and not by compulsion of God… For there is no coercion with God, but a good will [towards us] is present with Him continually."

"If then it were not in our power to do or not to do these things, what reason had the apostle, and much more the Lord Himself, to give us counsel to do some things, and to abstain from others? "…the Church, having received this preaching and this faith, although scattered throughout the whole world, yet, as if occupying but one house, carefully preserves it. She also believes these points of doctrine just as if she had but one soul, and one and the same heart…"

Irenaeus — a disciple of Polycarp, who was himself a disciple of the Apostle John — names all four: Matthew published a Gospel, Mark handed down Peter's preaching, Luke the companion of Paul recorded the Gospel Paul preached, and John the disciple of the Lord published his Gospel while residing at Ephesus.

q.quote: The righteous patriarchs before Moses "had the meaning of the Decalogue written in their hearts and souls."

q.quote: "Those who are in truth His disciples, receiving grace from Him, do in His name perform miracles... Others still, heal the sick by laying their hands upon them, and they are made whole. Yea, moreover, as I have said, the dead even have been raised up."

q.h: The Economic Trinity — God Revealed in History

q.h: VI.
q.quote: "As Eve was seduced by the word of an angel to flee from God, having rebelled against his Word, so Mary received the good tidings by means of the angel's word, so as to bear God, being obedient to his Word. And if the former did disobey God, yet the latter was persuaded to be obedient to God, in order that the Virgin Mary might become the patroness of the virgin Eve."

q.h: Against Despair — Why Fear Must Not Become Paralysis
q.quote: “Do not condemn even one who is a sinner; have compassion, and instead of condemning, say: ‘God, forgive both him and me.’” Isaac of Nineveh — a Syriac bishop and mystic technically of the Church of the East tradition, yet read and beloved across Oriental Orthodox, Eastern Orthodox, and Catholic monastic circles for over a millennium — taught that the sight of one’s own sin, rightly perceived, produces not despair but compassion — for oneself and for others. The person who knows their own need for mercy cannot despise another sinner.

q.h: The Afterlife
q.quote: "It is not the way of the compassionate Maker to create rational beings in order to deliver them over mercilessly to unending affliction… Those who say that God acts in hatred or severity toward sinners know him not."

q.h: Church Fathers on the Biblical Canon
q.quote: Jerome is the Father most cited by Protestants because he expressed preference for the Hebraica Veritas — the Hebrew truth — and noted that Judith, Tobit, Wisdom, and Sirach “are not in the canon.” Yet Jerome’s own practice contradicts his theory: he translated all these books into Latin for the Vulgate, included them in the Bible he produced for the Western church, and in his later commentaries quotes Sirach and Wisdom as scripture without qualification. Augustine sharply criticized his Hebrew-canon preference.

q.quote: "You say in your book that while we live we are able to pray for each other, but after death the prayer of no person for another can be heard, and this is especially so since the martyrs, though they cry for the avenging of their blood, have never been able to obtain their request. But if the apostles and martyrs while still in the body can pray for others, at a time when they ought still to be anxious about themselves, how much more will they do so after their crowns, victories, and triumphs?"

q.h: XIII.
q.quote: "Are we, whenever we ask for the prayers of the saints, to be charged with the crime of Saul? Saul went to consult a medium for hidden knowledge in defiance of God's law. We ask the holy ones to pray for us before the throne of God. If you charge us with necromancy, you must charge Paul with it as well, for he said 'pray for one another.'"

"And so these are somehow mixed up and indiscriminately confused… the grace of God always co-operates with our will for its advantage, and in all things assists, protects, and defends it, in such a way as sometimes even to require and look for some efforts of good will from it, that it may not appear to confer its gifts on one who is asleep."

"And so the grace of God always co-operates with our will for its advantage… These two then, viz., the grace of God and free will, seem opposed to each other, but really are in harmony, and we gather from the system of goodness that we ought to have both alike."

Chrysostom teaches that the bodies of the lost are raised incorruptible precisely so that the punishment does not end as this life's sufferings do: the fire of gehenna, unlike earthly fire, does not consume what it holds, and "that punishment has no end." He urges that one hour of it outweighs every pleasure of sin — and that God reveals it now exactly so that no one need ever see it.
q.cite: To Theodore After His Fall 1.10; Homilies on Matthew (c. AD 380–390)

q.h: Against Presumption — Why Certainty Can Be Dangerous
q.quote: “I do not think there are many among bishops that will be saved, but many more that perish; and the reason is, that it is an affair that requires a great mind. Many are the exigencies which throw a man out of his natural temper; and he had need of many eyes to see on all sides.” (Homily on Acts 3). Chrysostom — Archbishop of Constantinople, the greatest preacher of the ancient church — expressed genuine uncertainty about the salvation of bishops, including presumably himself. Not despair — but honest watchfulness.

q.h: Church Fathers on the Biblical Canon
q.quote: Chrysostom quotes Sirach throughout his homilies as inspired scripture, often prefacing quotations with “the Holy Spirit says through Sirach” or simply “scripture says.” His use of the book is so extensive and unreflective that modern scholars must note which “Sirach” references come from the canonical Psalms and which from Ecclesiasticus.

"God did not merely choose us, but chose us 'that we should be holy and without blemish.' … He chose us out of His own love — but our virtue also was required; for had holiness not depended also on us, the exhortations would have been superfluous."

"But the Manichaeans spring upon these words, saying that nothing lies in our own power; yet the expression shows that we are masters of our will. … This does not take away our free will, but shows that we greatly need help; and He brings not the unwilling, but draws him that has the will."
q.cite: Homilies on John 46, on John 6:44 (c. AD 390)

q.quote: Chrysostom treats the warning of Hebrews 6 as addressed to the baptized: there is one baptism and no second cross, therefore the Christian must not presume; yet he immediately guards against despair — repentance remains open to the fallen, for the "impossibility" concerns a second baptism, not the mercy of God toward the penitent.

"Why then are some vessels of wrath, and some of mercy? Of their own free choice. God, however, being very good, shows the same kindness to both. … It is not on the potter that the honour and the dishonour of the things made of the lump depends, but upon the use made by those who employ it; so here also it depends on the free choice."
q.cite: Homilies on Romans 16, on Rom. 9 (c. AD 391)

Chrysostom reads the veil not as a sign of inferiority but as a liturgical mark of dignity — the woman enters the holy of holies covered, as the priests were.

This is the mystery: each poured out for the other as Christ poured Himself out for the Church."

But what Paul means is that we are not justified by the works of the law — by circumcision and the sacrifices and festivals — but by grace through faith. This does not mean that works are not needed, but that the Mosaic rituals are not what save." PARAPHRASE

q.h: VII. The Church Fathers on Scripture and Tradition
q.quote: "Hence it is manifest that they did not deliver all things by letter, but there is much also that was not written. Like that also, the unwritten tradition is worthy of belief. So that we consider the tradition of the Church also to be worthy of belief. It is a tradition — seek no further."

q.h: IV. Tongues & Interpretation — The Most Contested Gift
q.quote: "This whole place is very obscure: but the obscurity is produced by our ignorance of the facts referred to and by their cessation, being such as then used to occur but now no longer take place." — Chrysostom acknowledges the diminishing of the spectacular manifestations while affirming the gifts in principle.

q.h: The Church as Body of Christ
q.quote: “You cannot have God as your Father if you refuse to have the Church as your mother. Membership in the Church is not a luxury but a necessity. The Body cannot live without its members, and the members cannot live apart from the Body.” (On the Epistle to the Ephesians 11). Chrysostom sees Christian isolation as a kind of spiritual suicide.

q.h: The Church as Body of Christ
q.quote: “Do you wish to honor the Body of Christ? Do not ignore him when he is naked. Do not pay him homage in the temple clad in silk, only then to neglect him outside where he is cold and ill-clad. He who said: ‘This is my body’ is the same who said: ‘You saw me hungry and you gave me no food.’” (Homily on Matthew 50.3). Chrysostom is merciless in connecting Eucharistic devotion with care for the poor — the two are not separate acts of piety but one continuous reality.

q.h: II.
q.quote: "Paul does not forbid women to teach altogether — for he teaches Titus that the older women must teach the younger women (Titus 2:3–4). He forbids them to teach in public, from the teacher's seat, with authority over men — this is what he restrains."

q.h: Church Fathers on Assurance
q.quote: “Before your fall, do not be proud. After your fall, do not despair.” (The Ladder of Divine Ascent 26). Climacus, a Byzantine abbot of Mt. Sinai, belongs formally to the Chalcedonian Eastern Orthodox tradition — yet his Ladder has been read in Coptic, Syriac, and Ethiopian monasteries for centuries. His teaching is included here as shared ascetic heritage. The ladder image rules out both static presumption (I’ve arrived) and hopeless despair (I’ve fallen too far) — a balance at the heart of all Orthodox spirituality.

q.h: Prayer and Spiritual Life
q.quote: "Do not try to be wordy in your prayer… one word of the publican propitiated God; one cry of faith saved the thief. Verbose prayer often distracts the mind."

So we worship expecting his coming.” (On the Orthodox Faith 4.13)

"In former times God, who is without form or body, could never be depicted. But now when God is seen in the flesh conversing with men, I make an image of the God whom I see. I do not worship matter; I worship the Creator of matter who became matter for my sake, who willed to take his abode in matter; who worked out my salvation through matter. Never will I cease honoring the matter which wrought my salvation!"
q.cite: First Treatise Against Those Who Attack the Divine Images (c. AD 730)

q.quote: "Every image reveals and represents something hidden. The Son is the image of the invisible Father, and the Spirit is the image of the Son. If you say that only intellectual things may be depicted, remove all corporate worship. Drive away all matter. For God created everything very good."

I do not worship matter; I worship the God of matter, who became matter for my sake, and deigned to inhabit matter, who worked out my salvation through matter."

"We use all our senses to produce worthy images of him, and we sanctify the noblest of the senses, which is that of sight. For just as words edify the ear, so also the image stimulates the eye. What the book is to the literate, the image is to the illiterate. Just as words speak to the hearing, so the image speaks to the sight; it brings us understanding."

"The Lawgiver commanded: 'You shall not make an idol.' But he also commanded the making of the Ark of the Covenant, the Cherubim, the mercy seat, the golden pot, Aaron's rod, and the veil embroidered with images. Are these idols? No — these were made for the purpose of worshipping and glorifying God, not for the worship of created things as God. The difference is not between images and no images. The difference is between images that lead to God and images that replace God."

q.quote: "I do not worship matter, I worship the God of matter, who became matter for my sake, and deigned to inhabit matter, who worked out my salvation through matter."

q.quote: "He was born of the Holy Spirit and of the ever-virgin Mary... She who before she gave birth was a virgin is still a virgin after the birth."

We do not worship the saints; we honor them. We do not offer them divine service; we entreat their intercession. The honor we give to the friends of God redounds to God himself, who is glorified in his saints."

q.cite: Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith I.8 — The systematic summary of patristic Trinitarian theology; still the standard theological text in the Orthodox East

q.quote: In his First Apology (c. 155 AD), Justin describes Christian practice to the pagan emperor: baptism, the weekly Eucharist, care for the poor, reading of the prophets and gospels, and the bishop’s presidency. He describes how Christians “who had been wealthier than before, bring what we have to the common fund, and distribute it to those who need.” Christian identity, for Justin, is inseparable from this communal, eucharistic, socially active life.

q.h: Before Chalcedon — The Road That Led There

q.h: Church Fathers on Assurance
q.quote: “The soul that has been granted the grace of the Spirit does not presume it is secure; it continues to run, to wrestle, to pray — always reaching for what is ahead, always aware of what is behind.” (Homilies 15). Macarius, one of the foundational Desert Fathers, describes the spiritually healthy Christian as one who never settles into complacency but presses forward — not from fear of rejection but from love of God.

q.h: Voices of the Fathers
q.quote: In the Egyptian desert tradition, Macarius is remembered teaching that the prayers of the Church bring some relief even to the souls of the lost — a witness to the early Coptic conviction that the living can aid the departed.

q.quote: At the Council of Ferrara–Florence he argued that the souls of the departed are aided by the Church's prayers and the Eucharist, but denied a purgatorial fire of punishment, holding that the Church had received no such teaching. He alone refused to sign the reunion.

q.quote: "Adam introduced the tyranny of pleasure-seeking over our nature, and after pleasure came pain, and after pain, death. We inherit not Adam's guilt but the nature he wounded — a nature enslaved to pleasure, pain, and death."

q.quote: "God and man are paradigms of each other, God humanizing himself for man's sake and man being deified for God's sake."

q.h: Salvation as Transformation — Theosis
q.quote: “A firm and reliable pledge of our hope for the deification of human nature is God’s Incarnation, which makes man god to the same degree as God himself became man. For clearly, the one who became man without sin will deify human nature without changing it into the divine nature, and will raise it up for his own sake.” (Ambigua 10). Maximus provides the most developed patristic theology of theosis, linking it directly to the Incarnation and the union of divine and human natures in Christ.

In his great paschal homily Melito presses the paradox of the Cross to its limit: He who hung the earth in place was hung; He who fixed the heavens was fixed to wood; God Himself was slain by the hand of Israel. The shock is deliberate — the Crucified is no mere man.

q.t: History of the Oriental Orthodox Church
